text:
FOUNDATION MEDICINE — FOUNDATIONONE CDx GENOMIC PROFILING REPORT

PATIENT INFORMATION
Patient Name: Marisol Fuentes-Delgado
Date of Birth: [REDACTED]
Sex: Female
Ordering Physician: [Oncologist Name]
Institution: [Cancer Center Name]
Diagnosis (per requisition): Metastatic triple-negative breast carcinoma, recurrence
Prior Oncologic Treatment History (per requisition): Neoadjuvant pembro + dose-dense doxo/cyclo (ddAC x4) + weekly PTX x12 weeks; adjuvant post-mastectomy radiation therapy; adjuvant Xeloda (8 cycles) + pembro maintenance (completed); metastatic recurrence confirmed

SPECIMEN INFORMATION
Specimen ID: [Institutional Lab ID]
Specimen Type: Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) core needle biopsy
Anatomic Site: Right hepatic lobe, segment VI (CT-guided percutaneous core needle biopsy)
Date of Procedure: [DATE]
Tumor Content (by pathologist review): 55% (adequate for sequencing)
Quality Control: All QC metrics passed. Median exon coverage depth: 842x. Sequence coverage uniformity >98%.

PRIOR NGS SPECIMEN (FOR COMPARISON)
Specimen Type: FFPE core needle biopsy, left breast primary tumor (12 o'clock position)
Prior Report Accession: [Prior Accession Number]
Note: Direct genomic comparison performed between primary tumor and current metastatic specimen.

---

SUMMARY OF GENOMIC FINDINGS

This specimen has been analyzed using the FoundationOne CDx assay, a Next Generation Sequencing (NGS)-based in vitro diagnostic device that uses targeted high-depth sequencing of 324 genes plus select intronic regions of 36 genes involved in rearrangements. Analysis detects substitutions, insertion-deletions, copy number alterations (amplifications and homozygous deletions), and rearrangements, as well as genomic signatures including tumor mutational burden (TMB) and microsatellite instability (MSI). This report has been augmented with full 500-gene panel data for comprehensive genomic profiling.

CLINICALLY RELEVANT GENOMIC ALTERATIONS (TIER I/II — ACTIONABLE OR POTENTIALLY ACTIONABLE)

1. TP53 p.R248W (c.742C>T)
   — Gene/Locus: TP53 (17p13.1)
   — Alteration Type: Missense substitution, hotspot mutation
   — Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 71% (compared to 68% in primary tumor; maintained)
   — Classification: Pathogenic (ClinVar, OncoKB Level 1 for loss-of-function context)
   — Functional Significance: Arginine-248 is the most frequently mutated codon in TP53 across all human cancers. The R248W substitution abolishes DNA contact with major groove binding, resulting in complete loss of transcriptional transactivation of canonical p53 targets (p21/CDKN1A, MDM2, PUMA, BAX). Additionally, this allele confers dominant-negative activity over wild-type p53 and gain-of-function oncogenic properties (enhanced invasion, drug resistance). This mutation was identified in the primary tumor and is maintained in the metastatic clone, consistent with early clonal driver status.
   — Therapeutic Relevance: No FDA-approved TP53-targeted therapies currently available in breast cancer; clinical trial eligibility may apply (APR-246/eprenetapopt for TP53 gain-of-function mutations; investigational).

2. BRCA1 — Biallelic Inactivation (Germline + Somatic LOH)
   — Gene/Locus: BRCA1 (17q21.31)
   — Germline Allele: c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756Profs*74); pathogenic frameshift duplication; confirmed in germline (patient known BRCA1 carrier)
   — Somatic Event: Loss of heterozygosity (LOH) at 17q21, consistent with deletion of the wild-type BRCA1 allele
   — VAF of Germline Allele in Tumor: ~71% (allele enrichment consistent with LOH)
   — Classification: Pathogenic; biallelic loss confirmed
   — Functional Significance: BRCA1 encodes a BRCA1-PALB2-BRCA2 complex member essential for homologous recombination DNA repair (HRR). Biallelic inactivation — via germline frameshift mutation plus somatic deletion of the wild-type copy — results in complete HRR deficiency. This is the dominant driver of genomic instability in this tumor, reflected in the high HRD score (see below).
   — Therapeutic Relevance: FDA-approved biomarker for PARP inhibitor sensitivity (ola [Lynparza], Talzenna [Talzenna]) in metastatic breast cancer (OlympiAD, EMBRACA data). Patient currently receiving Lynparza. Germline BRCA1 mutation also confers platinum sensitivity (cis, carbo) — note: patient has impaired renal function (CrCl ~49 mL/min), limiting platinum use.
   — Note: HRD maintained at metastatic site; no secondary BRCA1 reversion mutations detected (see Secondary/Resistance section below).

3. AKT1 p.E17K (c.49G>A) — NEWLY ACQUIRED IN METASTATIC SPECIMEN
   — Gene/Locus: AKT1 (14q32.33)
   — Alteration Type: Missense substitution, hotspot mutation
   — Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 28%
   — Status: NOT DETECTED in primary tumor NGS; newly acquired somatic mutation in metastatic clone
   — Classification: Pathogenic; hotspot mutation (COSMIC, OncoKB Level 3A)
   — Functional Significance: AKT1 p.E17K is one of the most recurrent oncogenic hotspot mutations in breast cancer. The glutamate-to-lysine substitution at position 17 within the PH domain constitutively activates AKT1 through enhanced membrane localization independent of upstream PI3K signaling. This bypasses the tumor suppressive effect of PTEN loss (see below) and activates downstream mTORC1/mTORC2, S6K1, 4EBP1, and FOXO pathway effectors, driving cell survival, proliferation, and anti-apoptotic signaling. The subclonal VAF of 28% suggests this mutation arose in a subpopulation of the primary tumor (below detection threshold) or was acquired de novo during metastatic progression; it likely represents a resistance mechanism to prior therapies, potentially including ola (PI3K/AKT pathway activation has been implicated in PARP inhibitor resistance in BRCA-mutated tumors).
   — Therapeutic Relevance: AKT inhibitors (capivasertib [Truqap] — FDA approved in combination with fulvestrant for HR+/HER2- breast cancer with AKT1/AKT2/AKT3 or PTEN-altered tumors; clinical trial eligibility for AKT1-mutant TNBC). Ipatasertib also investigated in TNBC (IPATunity130 trial). This acquired alteration may be clinically targetable upon disease progression.

4. PTEN — Homozygous Deletion
   — Gene/Locus: PTEN (10q23.31)
   — Alteration Type: Homozygous deletion (copy number = 0)
   — Status: Maintained from primary tumor
   — Classification: Pathogenic (tumor suppressor, loss of function)
   — Functional Significance: PTEN encodes a lipid phosphatase that antagonizes PI3K signaling by dephosphorylating PIP3 to PIP2. Homozygous deletion results in complete loss of PTEN protein and unrestrained PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway activation. In this tumor, co-occurring AKT1 p.E17K and PTEN loss represent convergent mechanisms of pathway activation, suggesting strong selective pressure for PI3K/AKT pathway dependency.
   — Therapeutic Relevance: Predictive biomarker for PI3K/AKT pathway inhibitors (capivasertib, ipatasertib). PTEN loss is an inclusion criterion in several clinical trials for PI3K pathway inhibitors in TNBC.

5. MYC — High-Level Amplification
   — Gene/Locus: MYC (8q24.21)
   — Alteration Type: Copy number amplification
   — Copy Number: 22.1 (further amplified compared to 18.4 in primary tumor)
   — Status: Maintained and increased from primary tumor
   — Classification: Pathogenic; high-level amplification
   — Functional Significance: MYC amplification drives transcriptional reprogramming, metabolic dysregulation, enhanced proliferation, and resistance to apoptosis. Increasing copy number from 18.4 (primary) to 22.1 (metastasis) suggests continued positive selection for MYC amplification during disease progression and possibly under treatment pressure. MYC amplification is associated with aggressive TNBC phenotype and has been linked to resistance to DNA-damaging chemotherapy.
   — Therapeutic Relevance: No approved direct MYC inhibitors; indirect targeting strategies under investigation (BET bromodomain inhibitors, CDK7 inhibitors). Aurora B kinase (AURKB) inhibitors may be relevant in MYC-amplified tumors.

6. CCND1 — Amplification — NEWLY ACQUIRED IN METASTATIC SPECIMEN
   — Gene/Locus: CCND1 (11q13.3)
   — Alteration Type: Copy number amplification
   — Copy Number: 8.2
   — Status: NOT DETECTED in primary tumor; newly acquired in metastatic clone
   — Classification: Potentially pathogenic; clinically actionable context
   — Functional Significance: CCND1 encodes cyclin D1, which complexes with CDK4/6 to drive G1-S cell cycle transition via RB1 phosphorylation and E2F transcription factor release. Paradoxically, CCND1 amplification in the context of co-existing RB1 loss (see below) may reflect alterations beyond classical CDK4/6-RB axis (e.g., CDK-independent cyclin D1 oncogenic functions including transcriptional co-activator roles, cytoplasmic functions). Alternatively, CCND1 amplification in a subclone with retained RB1 function may be present, given tumor heterogeneity. CCND1 amplification may represent a resistance mechanism to prior anthracycline-based therapy and/or contribute to PI3K pathway cross-talk via AKT1 activation.
   — Therapeutic Relevance: CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbo, ribo, abema) are approved in HR+ breast cancer; their role in TNBC with CCND1 amplification is investigational. RB1 status may modulate response (see RB1 below).

7. RB1 — Loss of Function
   — Gene/Locus: RB1 (13q14.2)
   — Alteration Type: p.L153Rfs*11 frameshift deletion (maintained) + 13q14 deletion (maintained)
   — Status: Maintained from primary tumor
   — VAF of Frameshift: ~69%
   — Classification: Pathogenic; biallelic inactivation strongly suspected
   — Functional Significance: RB1 is a critical cell cycle checkpoint tumor suppressor. Loss of RB1 results in uncontrolled cell cycle progression. In the context of co-occurring CCND1 amplification, this suggests possible intratumoral heterogeneity with distinct subclones, or that CCND1 amplification exerts its oncogenic effects through RB1-independent mechanisms (e.g., cytoplasmic/transcriptional co-activator roles).
   — Therapeutic Relevance: RB1 loss predicts resistance to CDK4/6 inhibitors and is a negative predictive biomarker. Paradoxically may sensitize to CDK2 inhibitors or other targeted strategies.

8. CDKN2A/CDKN2B — Co-Deletion
   — Gene/Locus: CDKN2A/CDKN2B (9p21.3)
   — Alteration Type: Homozygous co-deletion (copy number = 0 at both loci)
   — Status: Maintained from primary tumor
   — Classification: Pathogenic; loss of both p16INK4A and p14ARF (CDKN2A) and p15INK4B (CDKN2B)
   — Functional Significance: Loss of CDKN2A ablates both p16INK4A (CDK4/6 inhibitor) and p14ARF (MDM2 inhibitor/TP53 stabilizer). Combined with TP53 mutation, this represents total disruption of both major G1/S cell cycle checkpoints and the ARF-MDM2-TP53 tumor suppressor pathway. CDKN2B loss further amplifies CDK4/6 activity.

---

ADDITIONAL SOMATIC MUTATIONS — VARIANTS OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE (VUS) AND SELECTED FINDINGS

- NF1 p.D1644N (VUS): Maintained from primary tumor. NF1 encodes neurofibromin, a RAS-GAP protein. This variant is of uncertain significance; if functional, could activate RAS/MAPK signaling. Tumor has no KRAS/NRAS mutations.
- KDM6A p.R479C (VUS): Maintained. KDM6A is a histone H3K27 demethylase; loss may alter chromatin state. Uncertain therapeutic significance.
- ZFHX3 p.D1636N (VUS): Maintained. Transcription factor; uncertain significance in breast cancer.
- RNF43 p.G659Vfs*41 (truncating, VUS): Maintained. RNF43 is a negative regulator of Wnt signaling; truncating mutations may activate Wnt pathway; porcupine inhibitors could be of interest if Wnt dependency confirmed.
- SPEN p.R2196H (VUS): Maintained. SPEN (SHARP) is involved in transcriptional repression and has been implicated in ER signaling modulation (not relevant given ER-negative phenotype).
- FAT3 p.S2152L (VUS): Maintained. FAT3 is an atypical cadherin; uncertain significance.
- RAD51C p.P34R (VUS): NEW in metastatic specimen (not detected in primary tumor). RAD51C is a paralogue of RAD51, essential for homologous recombination. This variant of uncertain significance could potentially affect HRR pathway function; classification as likely benign versus pathogenic pending functional studies. No impact on HRD score calculation.
- ARID1A p.Q330* (truncating, previously likely pathogenic): NOT DETECTED in metastatic specimen (detected at VAF ~22% in primary tumor). Consistent with clonal evolution — ARID1A-mutant subclone was not selected during metastatic progression, or underwent further tumor evolution. Loss of ARID1A had been associated with sensitivity to ATR inhibitors and HDAC inhibitors; its absence in the metastatic clone may reduce relevance of these strategies.

---

COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS — COMPREHENSIVE SUMMARY

| Locus        | Gene(s)          | Type                  | Copy Number | Prior (Primary) |
|--------------|------------------|-----------------------|-------------|------------------|
| 8q24.21      | MYC              | High-level amplif.   | 22.1        | 18.4             |
| 10q23.31     | PTEN             | Homozygous deletion   | 0           | 0                |
| 9p21.3       | CDKN2A/CDKN2B    | Homozygous co-del.    | 0           | 0                |
| 13q14.2      | RB1              | Deletion              | ~1 (hemi)   | ~1 (hemi)        |
| 1q32.1       | MDM4             | Amplification         | 5.8         | ~6.1             |
| 11q13.3      | CCND1            | Amplification (new)   | 8.2         | Not detected     |
| 17q21.31     | BRCA1            | LOH (germline allele enriched) | N/A | N/A          |
| 17p13.1      | TP53             | No copy change        | 2           | 2                |
| 14q32.33     | AKT1             | No copy change        | 2           | 2                |

Genome-wide LOH: 31% (increased from 24% in primary tumor; consistent with ongoing genomic instability and clonal evolution during therapy).

---

GENE FUSIONS / REARRANGEMENTS

No clinically significant gene fusions or structural rearrangements detected.
NTRK1/NTRK2/NTRK3: No rearrangements detected.
FGFR1/FGFR2/FGFR3/FGFR4: No rearrangements or amplifications detected.
ALK/ROS1/RET: No rearrangements detected (included for comprehensive profiling).
ERBB2 (HER2): No amplification, no activating mutation detected.

---

GENOMIC SIGNATURES

Microsatellite Status (MSI):
— MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
— MSI score: 0.9 (threshold for MSI-high: >10)
— No evidence of mismatch repair deficiency.
— Therapeutic Relevance: MSS status predicts reduced likelihood of response to immune checkpoint blockade as monotherapy (relative to MSI-high tumors). Note: patient's prior PD-L1 CPS 28 led to pembro use in KEYNOTE-522 protocol context.

Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB):
— TMB: 11 mutations/Mb (intermediate; slightly increased from 9 mut/Mb in primary tumor)
— TMB-high threshold: ≥10 mut/Mb (per FDA TMB-H approval for pembro in solid tumors)
— Classification: TMB-INTERMEDIATE to HIGH (borderline)
— Therapeutic Relevance: Patient has prior exposure to Keytruda. TMB elevation may be partly attributed to mutational signature contributions from prior cytotoxic therapy (see SBS17, below).

Homologous Recombination Deficiency (HRD) Score:
— HRD SCORE: 68 (HIGH; threshold for HRD-high: ≥42)
— Genomic Instability Index components:
     • LOH score: 22 (high)
     • Telomeric allelic imbalance (TAI): 28 (high)
     • Large-scale state transitions (LST): 18 (high)
— Interpretation: Maintained high HRD score is consistent with ongoing BRCA1-deficient HR repair deficiency. No reversion mutations detected in BRCA1 (see below). HRD-high status supports continued PARP inhibitor sensitivity in principle, although acquired AKT1 mutation may represent an emerging resistance mechanism.
— Therapeutic Relevance: Supports continued use of Lynparza (current therapy). Platinum sensitivity also predicted (caution: renal function limits this option).

Mutational Signature Analysis:
— DOMINANT SIGNATURE: SBS3 (Defective homologous recombination repair / BRCA-associated)
     Proportion: ~58% of somatic mutations
     Interpretation: Consistent with BRCA1 biallelic inactivation and HRD. Maintained from primary tumor as dominant signature.
— SECONDARY SIGNATURE: SBS17b (associated with 5FU/fluoropyrimidine exposure)
     Proportion: ~21% of somatic mutations
     Interpretation: NEW contribution not observed in primary tumor analysis. Consistent with prior Xeloda (prodrug of 5-FU) exposure in adjuvant setting. SBS17 is characterized by T>G transversions with specific trinucleotide context enrichment.
— TERTIARY SIGNATURE: SBS2 + SBS13 (APOBEC cytosine deaminase activity)
     Combined proportion: ~14% of somatic mutations
     Interpretation: Mild APOBEC signature contribution; not dominant but present, consistent with biology of BRCA1-deficient triple-negative breast cancer.
— SBS1 (age-related, spontaneous deamination): ~7% of somatic mutations

---

BRCA REVERSION MUTATION ANALYSIS

High-depth analysis of BRCA1 exon 11 (c.5266 locus) was performed specifically to detect potential reversion mutations that restore BRCA1 reading frame — a well-characterized mechanism of PARP inhibitor and platinum resistance.
— RESULT: NO BRCA1 REVERSION MUTATIONS DETECTED at or surrounding c.5266dupC at this sequencing depth.
— Interpretation: PARP inhibitor resistance via BRCA1 reversion is not the mechanism operative at this time point. The AKT1 p.E17K and CCND1 amplification are more likely contributors to any emerging resistance.

---

COMPARATIVE GENOMIC EVOLUTION — PRIMARY TUMOR vs. METASTATIC SPECIMEN

ALTERATIONS MAINTAINED (CLONAL DRIVER MUTATIONS):
— TP53 p.R248W (VAF 68% → 71%): Stable clonal driver
— BRCA1 biallelic inactivation: Stable; no reversion
— PTEN homozygous deletion: Maintained
— RB1 loss: Maintained
— CDKN2A/CDKN2B co-deletion: Maintained
— MYC amplification (18.4 → 22.1 copies): Maintained and increased; further positive selection during progression
— MDM4 amplification: Maintained (~6 copies)
— NF1, KDM6A, ZFHX3, RNF43, SPEN, FAT3 VUS mutations: Maintained

NEWLY ACQUIRED ALTERATIONS (METASTATIC/TREATMENT-EMERGENT):
— AKT1 p.E17K (VAF 28%): Not detected in primary; likely subclonal emergence or acquired resistance mechanism
— CCND1 amplification (8.2 copies): Not detected in primary; possible acquired resistance to DNA damage, cell cycle dysregulation
— RAD51C p.P34R (VUS): Not detected in primary; uncertain significance
— SBS17 mutational signature: Treatment-related (5-FU/cape exposure imprint)
— Increased genome-wide LOH (24% → 31%): Progressive genomic instability

LOST ALTERATIONS (CLONAL EVOLUTION / SUBCLONE EXTINCTION):
— ARID1A p.Q330* (truncating, likely pathogenic): Detected at VAF ~22% in primary; NOT DETECTED in metastatic specimen. This subclone was likely non-dominant and did not contribute to the metastatic clone, consistent with Darwinian clonal selection during neoadjuvant therapy and metastatic outgrowth.

---

FDA-APPROVED COMPANION DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS

| Biomarker             | Result                         | FDA-Approved Drug(s) in Relevant Context              |
|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|
| BRCA1 germline + LOH | Pathogenic (biallelic)         | ola (Lynparza) — metastatic breast cancer (approved) |
| BRCA1               | Pathogenic                     | Talzenna (Talzenna) — metastatic breast cancer (approved) |
| MSI                  | MSS                            | pembro (Keytruda) — NOT indicated (MSS)        |
| TMB                  | 11 mut/Mb (borderline high)    | Keytruda (Keytruda) — TMB-H ≥10 mut/Mb          |
| PIK3CA               | Wild-type                      | Alpelisib (Piqray) — NOT indicated (PIK3CA WT)        |
| NTRK fusions         | Not detected                   | Larotrectinib, entrectinib — NOT indicated            |
| HER2                 | Not amplified, not mutated     | trast and related — NOT indicated               |
| ESR1                 | Not detected                   | Elacestrant — NOT indicated                           |
| AKT1 p.E17K (new)    | Detected (VAF 28%)             | Capivasertib (Truqap) — approved in HR+/HER2- with AKT pathway alteration; TNBC use investigational |

---

CLINICAL TRIAL ELIGIBILITY — SELECTED RELEVANT TRIALS

Based on genomic profile, the following trial types may be relevant for future treatment planning:
1. AKT inhibitor trials in TNBC with AKT1 mutation/PTEN loss (e.g., ipatasertib + chemotherapy, capivasertib + sacituzumab govitecan combinations)
2. PARP inhibitor combinations and sequencing strategies in BRCA1-mutated TNBC
3. PI3K/mTOR pathway targeting in PTEN-null TNBC
4. MYC-directed strategies (BET inhibitors, CDK7 inhibitors) — investigational
5. Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 rechallenge given prior exposure (note: PD-L1 CPS 28 confirmed on primary; metastatic site PD-L1 not re-tested on this specimen)

---

TECHNICAL NOTES AND ASSAY LIMITATIONS

— Assay: FoundationOne CDx (Foundation Medicine, Inc.), CLIA-certified, CAP-accredited, FDA-approved for solid tumor genomic profiling
— Sequencing platform: Illumina HiSeq (hybrid capture, paired-end)
— Panel coverage: 324 cancer-related genes (full exonic coverage); 36 genes with intron coverage for fusion detection; whole-genome copy number analysis; TMB and MSI calculation from sequence data
— This report includes extended 500-gene panel data for research purposes
— Sensitivity: Single nucleotide variants and small insertions/deletions ≥5% allele frequency in adequate tumor content specimens; copy number amplifications ≥6 copies; homozygous deletions ≥1 exon; fusions in select gene pairs
— Germline contamination: This is a tumor-only assay; germline variants (e.g., BRCA1 c.5266dupC) may be detected but cannot be definitively classified as somatic or germline without a paired normal specimen. Germline status confirmed by prior dedicated germline testing.
— Limitations: This assay does not detect all genomic alterations. Certain large structural variants, epigenetic alterations, and non-coding mutations are not captured. Tumor heterogeneity may result in subclonal variants below detection threshold.
— All alterations are interpreted in the context of known cancer biology, published literature, and curated databases (OncoKB, ClinVar, COSMIC, CIViC).

---

ATTENDING PATHOLOGIST GENOMIC REVIEW: [Name, MD], Molecular Pathology
GENOMIC ONCOLOGIST REVIEW: [Name, MD/PhD], Precision Oncology
Report verified and authorized by: [Name]

This report is intended to be interpreted by a qualified physician in the context of the patient's full clinical history. Therapeutic decisions should not be based solely on this report.

[END OF REPORT]
